goal_spec_json.goal_statement: Sleep a minimum of 6 hours consistently every night

full_morning_briefing.definition: Evaluate the morning_message together with the same-day generated_actions, because both are shown to the user as one morning briefing experience.
full_morning_briefing.morning_message:
Good morning Ameena — 89/100 this morning tells me you're feeling strong and ready.

Last night was a recovery win: **9.55 hours** of deep, efficient sleep (88% efficiency) and your HRV hit a solid 108 — your body absolutely recharged. That's the good news. The twist is that you fell asleep after 11:30 PM, continuing a really volatile sleep schedule. Meanwhile, yesterday told a different story: your stress was high at 69/100, your coping capacity dropped to 44, and you felt Annoyed and Tense. That's a classic **stress-recovery dissociation** — your physiology is strong, but your mind is carrying a heavy load.

Here's where it connects: yesterday you were also extremely sedentary (15 hours sitting) with zero moderate activity and only 1866 steps. When high stress meets prolonged stillness, it often creates a spiral — the body recovers but the mind doesn't get the movement it needs to process tension.

Today is about bridging that gap. You have this great physiological foundation from last night, so let's use that energy to gently shift both your movement patterns and your stress response.

The actions today focus on simple, rhythmic breaks — a walk to disrupt the sedentary pattern, breathing to intercept stress spikes, and an evening screen boundary to protect your sleep timing.

You've got the recovery fuel in the tank; today's about directing it toward movement and calm.

generated_actions.title: Take a 12-minute brisk walk after lunch
fallbacks: If weather prevents outdoor walking, walk indoors for 12 minutes (around your home/office)
If 12 minutes feels too long, do 8 minutes but maintain brisk pace
generated_actions.rationale: YESTERDAY's activity was critically poor with 0 MVPA, only 1866 steps (-556 slope_fast decline), and 900 sedentary minutes. This directly counters the sedentary stress spiral identified in the holistic report, disrupts the sharp decline in movement, and leverages your high subjective energy (89) to add moderate-effort movement that fits within your tight schedule. The 12-minute duration is specific and achievable.

evaluation.success_definition: Today's step count exceeds yesterday's critically low 1866 steps, and at least some MVPA minutes are recorded (breaking the 0 MVPA streak).

assumptions: You have safe walking routes available
You can spare 12 minutes after lunch
generated_actions.description: When you finish your lunch today, immediately put on your shoes and walk outside for exactly 12 minutes at a brisk pace. Focus on moving your body rather than distance.

generated_actions.title: When you feel tension rising, do 3 rounds of box breathing
fallbacks: If 3 rounds feels too long, do just 1 complete cycle (16 seconds)
If you forget during stress, do it as soon as you remember
generated_actions.rationale: YESTERDAY's subjective state showed high stress (69) with low coping capacity (44) and negative emotions (Annoyed, Tense), with a 25-point stress-coping gap. The stress-recovery dissociation signal shows psychological stress isn't aligned with your strong physiological recovery. This implementation intention provides a concrete tool to interrupt the stress spiral when it occurs, addressing the 'address high stress and low coping capacity gap' soft constraint directly.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you use the box breathing technique when you felt stress or tension today?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports intentionally using the breathing technique at least once when noticing stress or tension, creating a brief pause in the stress response.

assumptions: You can safely pause briefly during work
You can breathe comfortably
generated_actions.description: When you notice feelings of annoyance, tension, or stress building during work, immediately pause and perform 3 cycles of box breathing: inhale 4 seconds, hold 4 seconds, exhale 4 seconds, hold 4 seconds. Focus only on your breath.

generated_actions.title: Set a 9:45 PM 'digital sunset' alarm and read a physical book for 15 minutes
fallbacks: If 9:45 PM is too early, set for 10:00 PM but commit to the screen boundary
If no book available, write in a journal or do light stretching in dim light
generated_actions.rationale: LAST NIGHT's sleep was excellent in duration (9.55h) and efficiency (88.02%) but occurred at an extremely late and volatile time (23:31 onset, +128 slope_fast volatility). This screen boundary directly addresses the 'volatile sleep schedule with strong recovery' cross-domain signal, reinforces the 'prioritize wind-down routine before late bedtime' soft constraint, and serves your goal domain of sleep by creating structure to prevent repeat of extreme schedule instability while preserving sleep quality.

signal_refs.label: sleep onset earlier than last night's 23:31

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you honor the 9:45 PM screen boundary and read for 15 minutes?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports avoiding screens after 9:45 PM and engaging in screen-free reading, aiming for earlier sleep onset than last night's extremely late 23:31 to address schedule volatility.

assumptions: You have a smartphone with alarms
You have access to non-screen reading material
You have dim lighting available
generated_actions.description: When your phone alarm goes off at 9:45 PM, immediately put away all screens (phone, laptop, TV). Spend the next 15 minutes reading a physical book or magazine under a soft lamp or dim light.

generated_actions.title: Set a 50-minute 'stand and stretch' timer during your deepest work period
fallbacks: If timer is disruptive, take the break after completing a natural task
If standing isn't possible, do seated shoulder rolls and neck stretches
generated_actions.rationale: YESTERDAY's critically low activity (1866 steps, 0 MVPA) coincided with 900 sedentary minutes and rising subjective stress—a sedentary stress spiral. This implementation intention creates rhythmic breaks to disrupt prolonged sitting during your 'high workload' and 'tight schedule', supports focus (which was low at 38 yesterday), and provides micro-movement opportunities without requiring significant time commitment.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you use the 50-minute timer to take a standing stretch break during work?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports setting the timer and taking at least one standing stretch break when it chimed, disrupting prolonged sitting.

assumptions: You have a timer/phone
You can pause work briefly
You can stand and stretch safely
generated_actions.description: During your most focused work block today, set a timer for 50 minutes. When it chimes, stand up immediately, walk to a window or different room, and do 30 seconds of light stretching (reach for ceiling, side bends).

previous_day_generated_actions.title: Set a strict 2pm caffeine cutoff and a 10pm 'prepare for bed' alarm
fallbacks: If 2pm is too early, set cutoff for 3pm but commit to it.
If you miss the 10pm alarm, start wind-down as soon as you remember.
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: LAST NIGHT's sleep onset was extremely late (23:47), 12 fast-scale deviations above baseline, continuing severe schedule volatility. Yesterday's caffeine (65mg) was not after 2pm—keep that positive pattern. Reinforcing a strict morning/early afternoon caffeine cutoff and anchoring a pre-sleep ritual to a 10pm alarm directly addresses the 'volatility' risk flag and the 'reinforce early caffeine cutoff' and 'prioritize wind-down routine' soft constraints. This builds on your goal domain of sleep by creating structure to prevent repeat of last night's late onset.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you honor the 2pm caffeine cutoff and start your screen-free wind-down at 10pm?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports no caffeine after 2pm and initiating a screen-free wind-down by 10pm, aiming to stabilize tonight's sleep onset earlier than last night's extreme 23:47.

assumptions: You have a smartphone with alarms
You have access to caffeine-free beverages
You have a quiet, dimly lit space for wind-down
previous_day_generated_actions.description: When your phone alarm goes off at 2pm, have your last caffeinated drink. At 10pm, set a second alarm to start your wind-down: put away screens, dim lights, and begin a quiet activity like reading.

previous_day_generated_actions.title: At your first evening meal, double the protein portion from yesterday's dinner
fallbacks: If no whole protein available, add a scoop of protein powder to a drink or have a large handful of nuts.
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: YESTERDAY's nutrition was 'critical' with total intake very low (991 kcal) and protein sharply declining (-10.32 slope_fast, 53.7g vs. 84.66 baseline). Your dinner was a boiled egg and yogurt—a good base. Doubling that portion directly counters the steep decline, addresses the 'nutrition_gap' risk flag and 'low energy intake' cross-domain signal, and supports physiological recovery (proxy 36) by providing essential building blocks. This is meal-aware, referencing yesterday's actual foods to promote consistency.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you intentionally include a double protein portion with your first meal?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports adding a substantial protein source to their evening meal, and today's total protein reaches at least 70g (improving on yesterday's critically low 53.7g).

assumptions: You are observing Ramadan with an evening meal
You have access to eggs, yogurt, or another protein source
previous_day_generated_actions.description: When you break your fast tonight, include two boiled eggs or a double serving of Greek yogurt alongside your meal, aiming for at least 25g of protein in that sitting.

must_avoid: relying only on carbs/sweets like yesterday's chocolate petit fours

previous_day_generated_actions.title: Take a 7-minute 'reset walk' after your deepest work block
fallbacks: If you can't walk, stand by a window and do 3 minutes of light stretching or marching in place.
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: YESTERDAY's activity was 'critical' with 0 MVPA, low steps (2850), and high sedentary time (788 min) trending upward. A timed, intentional walk disrupts the sedentary spiral, provides a cognitive reset (your focus is high at 84), and supports physiological recovery by increasing blood flow. It fits within day constraints by being brief and anchored to a work transition, differing from recent marching or shoulder-roll suggestions.

evaluation.success_definition: Today's step count exceeds yesterday's 2850, indicating you added light movement despite a 'tight schedule' and 'high workload'.

assumptions: You can step away from work briefly
You can walk safely indoors or outdoors
previous_day_generated_actions.description: When you finish your most demanding work task or hit a natural stopping point, step outside (or walk indoors) for exactly 7 minutes at a gentle pace. Focus on your breathing and surroundings.

must_avoid: checking your phone during the walk

previous_day_generated_actions.title: Perform a 5-minute body scan while lying in bed before sleep
fallbacks: If 5 minutes feels long, do a 2-minute version focusing only on feet, hands, and shoulders.
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: LAST NIGHT's sleep had low REM (15.25%) and you report a persistent 8-day subjective-objective mismatch (gap 35). A body scan promotes parasympathetic activation and somatic awareness, which may improve sleep architecture and help bridge the perception-recovery gap. It addresses the 'mismatch_pattern' risk flag and serves your sleep goal by providing a non-screen, calming pre-sleep ritual, varying from recent breathing or reading actions.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you complete the 5-minute body scan as the first activity upon getting into bed?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports performing the body scan exercise immediately upon lying down, using it as a deliberate transition to sleep.

assumptions: You can lie down safely
You have a quiet environment
previous_day_generated_actions.description: When you get into bed tonight, lie on your back and spend 5 minutes mentally scanning your body from toes to head, noticing any tension and consciously relaxing each part. Breathe naturally.

previous_day_briefing.morning_message:
Morning Ameena.

You said you're at a 71 with high energy this morning — that's a solid place to start, especially considering what's happening underneath. Last night you got 6.95 hours of sleep, which is just under your baseline, but you fell asleep extremely late at 11:47pm — that's the biggest shift we've seen in weeks. The bigger story is what your body's telling us: your recovery proxy dropped to 36, your HRV is down to 78, and your resting heart rate climbed to 65. These are all clear signs your system is under real physiological strain, even though you *feel* resilient. That's the 8th day in a row where your subjective energy has been running ahead of what your body can actually sustain.

Yesterday you also had critically low nutrition (991 calories, 53g protein) and zero exercise — when energy intake is that low and activity is that minimal, it creates a double deficit that makes recovery even harder. The late sleep onset and early first meal at 4:20am point to your Ramadan rhythm, but it's creating a compounding effect where sleep debt and energy debt are stacking up.

Today is about breaking that cycle — giving your body what it needs to actually match how you feel.

We're focusing on stabilizing your sleep schedule and rebuilding your energy reserves with intentional protein and movement.

You've shown incredible mental resilience for 8 straight days — today's about giving your physiology the same support.

evidence_quotes: I spent the whole day in front of screens and my eyes were hurting
My exam also didnt go very well

meal_descriptions.description: One brown toast with labneh and one brown toast with cheddar cheese and instant coffee
Karak tea and 2 sugar covered baked sweets
2 large spoons of macaroni with red sauce, 1 chicken pane and 1 beef escalope and a handful of fries
